title: Treating bumblefoot in chickens
subtitle: How to identify and manage the most common foot infection in laying hens.
excerpt: A chicken with bumblefoot holds the affected foot higher than usual or has a noticeable limp. Caught early, the condition responds well to daily cleaning and bandaging at home. Left untreated, the infection spreads to the bone and becomes very difficult to resolve.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). University of Wisconsin Extension, poultry health fact sheets. RSPCA guidance on poultry foot conditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bacterial infection of the foot pad in poultry, typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus entering through a small wound or abrasion. Presents as a hard, dark scab on the sole of the foot, sometimes with swelling. Caused by rough perch surfaces, wet bedding, and high jumps from roosting height.

content.text: (plantar pododermatitis) is one of the most common health problems in backyard hens. It is preventable and treatable in its early stages. The key is regular foot inspections and prompt action when you see a scab forming.

Hold the hen with her feet visible. Look at the sole of each foot. A grade 1 infection is a small, dry, black or dark-brown scab on the pad with no surrounding swelling (early and manageable at home). A grade 3 infection involves significant swelling of the foot or leg, hot tissue, and possibly a discharge, and needs a vet.

For a grade 1 infection with a simple scab and no swelling: soak the foot in warm water for 15 minutes to soften the scab. Clean the area with a dilute antiseptic solution. Apply a small pad of gauze and a clean bandage around the foot, taped or secured with vet wrap. Change the bandage every other day. In many cases the infection resolves in two to three weeks without surgery or drugs.

attrs.title: When to see a vet
attrs.body: If the foot is significantly swollen, if the swelling extends to the joint or leg, if the hen stops eating or loses weight, or if there is no improvement after 10 days of home treatment, take her to a vet. Deeper grade 2 or 3 infections require professional debridement or a course of antibiotics; only a vet can prescribe these.

content.text: Address the cause as well as the infection. Check perch surfaces for splinters and sharp edges; sand them smooth. Lower the perch height if the hens are jumping from more than about 60 cm to a hard surface. Keep bedding dry; wet, compacted litter is the main environmental cause. Add extra bedding under roosting areas where droppings accumulate.
